intro: 2003. The GameCube is a hard third behind Sony's PS2 and Microsoft's Xbox. Everyone KNOWS the rule: win by having the strongest console. You run Nintendo. You're about to look at that rule and say 'nah.'

realOutcome.summary: Nintendo stopped fighting on horsepower and turned gaming into a thing everyone does. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: You're losing the console war on raw power. Pour everything into out-speccing Sony next gen — or stop fighting for the same sweaty hardcore gamers and grow WHO plays games entirely.

choices.text: Expand the audience. Stop fighting on horsepower.
choices.reveal: Iwata went 'blue ocean': instead of chasing Sony's hardcore crowd, grow the market to people who never touched a controller. Rewrote the entire competition. Galaxy-brain move.

choices.text: Out-spec Sony with a raw power console
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You brawl over teraflops and lose on price, dev support, and exclusives. Third place in a war you chose to fight on the enemy's home turf. Ego death.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You give up the platform control that lets Mario and Zelda sell consoles. Nintendo becomes just another studio living on other people's boxes. Sad.

decisions.scenario: For your next handheld, Sony's bringing the PSP — sleek, powerful, media beast. You have a weird idea: two screens, one touch-controlled, marketed as a whole new 'third pillar.'

choices.reveal: The DS, with its touchscreen and weird little games, became one of the best-selling systems ever — 150M+ units — and absolutely buried the 'more powerful' PSP. Weird wins.

choices.text: Build a straightforward beefier Game Boy
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A better Game Boy fights the PSP on the PSP's terms — specs and media. You gain ground but never crack open a brand-new audience. Meh.

choices.text: Cede handhelds to Sony, focus on consoles
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You abandon the ONE market you dominate. Sony takes portables and you've handed away your safest, richest turf. Why would you do this.

decisions.scenario: Next console, your team pitches motion controls, a weak GPU, and a low price — codename 'Revolution.' It won't do cutting-edge graphics and hardcore reviewers will absolutely roast it.

choices.text: Bet on motion controls and a cheap weak box
choices.reveal: The Wii launched in 2006 with motion controls and potato hardware — and outsold the way-more-powerful PS3 and Xbox 360 for YEARS, landing in living rooms that never owned a console. Certified phenomenon.

choices.text: Build a graphics powerhouse to match the rivals
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You match Sony and Microsoft on power, sell at a loss like them, and dissolve into a three-way spec war. Grandma never buys a console. No magic.

choices.text: Ship a safe HD console with a normal controller
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A competent, forgettable box. No motion gimmick means no cultural moment, no Wii Sports mania, no new audience. Mid incarnate.

decisions.scenario: Sony and Microsoft sell their consoles at a LOSS to grab share, making it back on games. You can price the Wii to actually profit on every unit at $250.

choices.text: Price at $250, profitable from unit one
choices.reveal: Cheaper hardware meant Nintendo profited on every single Wii from day one — nearly unheard of. It printed money while rivals bled on each box. Silent flex.

choices.text: Sell at a loss to max out install base
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You copy the rivals' loss-leader math and toss away the Wii's biggest financial edge. Growth you overpay for. Why.

choices.text: Price it premium at $400 to signal quality
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A $400 underpowered console is a rough sell, chief. The mass-market buyers you were counting on stay home.

decisions.scenario: Market the Wii to the usual hardcore gamers, or aim straight at families, grandparents, and total newbies — bundling a simple game called Wii Sports so anyone can pick it up in seconds.

choices.text: Target families and non-gamers, bundle Wii Sports
choices.reveal: Nintendo bundled Wii Sports and marketed to literally everyone, kids to grandparents. It became a social phenomenon and one of the best-selling games of all time. Everyone's mom had a Wii.

choices.text: Market only to core gamers like the rivals
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You scrap for the same hardcore buyers with weaker hardware. The audience that made the Wii a cultural event never gets the invite.

choices.text: Skip the pack-in game to protect software sales
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: No Wii Sports in the box, so first-timers have nothing that instantly shows what this thing DOES. The motion magic stays trapped on the store shelf.

bands.line: You changed the game instead of losing it. Legend episode: WON. No notes.
A solid third-place console maker. Alive, never the phenomenon. Mid.
You fought the spec war and lost it. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Nintendo 5x'd by refusing the arms race. Today's agents flagged a company winning a game nobody else is playing →
